section.title: The Olivet Discourse: Signs of the End
section.sectionRationale: The fifth and final discourse answers the disciples' question about the temple's fall and the end of the age. Jesus warns against deception, foretells tribulation and the destruction of Jerusalem, promises His glorious return, and calls for constant, faithful watchfulness because no one knows the day or hour.
section.thesis: History runs through wars, deception, persecution, and the fall of Jerusalem toward the certain, visible, glorious return of the Son of Man; since the day and hour are unknown, His people must not be deceived but persevere, keep watch, and serve faithfully until He comes.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: the temple's fall foretold and the disciples' question; the beginning of birth pains and the worldwide witness; the great tribulation; the coming of the Son of Man; the lesson of the fig tree and this generation; the unknown day and the call to watch; and the faithful versus the wicked servant.

units.label: The question on the Mount of Olives

verses.text: As Jesus left the temple and was walking away, His disciples came up to Him to point out its buildings.
“Do you see all these things?” He replied. “Truly I tell you, not one stone here will be left on another; every one will be thrown down.”

While Jesus was sitting on the Mount of Olives, the disciples came to Him privately. “Tell us,” they said, “when will these things happen, and what will be the sign of Your {{parousia|coming}} and of the end of the age?”
Jesus answered, “See to it that no one deceives you.
For many will come in My name, claiming, ‘I am the Christ,’ and will deceive many.
You will hear of wars and rumors of wars, but see to it that you are not alarmed. These things must happen, but the end is still to come.
Nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom. There will be famines and earthquakes in various places.
All these are the beginning of birth pains.
Then they will deliver you over to be persecuted and killed, and you will be hated by all nations because of My name.
At that time many will fall away and will betray and hate one another,
and many false prophets will arise and deceive many.
Because of the multiplication of wickedness, the love of most will grow cold.
But the one who perseveres to the end will be saved.
And this gospel of the kingdom will be preached in all the world as a testimony to all nations, and then the end will come.
So when you see standing in the holy place ‘the abomination of desolation,’ spoken of by the prophet Daniel (let the reader understand),
then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains.
Let no one on the housetop come down to retrieve anything from his house.
And let no one in the field return for his cloak.
How miserable those days will be for pregnant and nursing mothers!
Pray that your flight will not occur in the winter or on the Sabbath.
For at that time there will be great {{thlipsis|tribulation}}, unseen from the beginning of the world until now, and never to be seen again.
If those days had not been cut short, nobody would be saved. But for the sake of the elect, those days will be cut short.
At that time, if anyone says to you, ‘Look, here is the Christ!’ or ‘There He is!’ do not believe it.
For false Christs and false prophets will appear and perform great signs and wonders to deceive even the elect, if that were possible.
See, I have told you in advance.
So if they tell you, ‘There He is, in the wilderness,’ do not go out, or, ‘Here He is, in the inner rooms,’ do not believe it.
For just as the lightning comes from the east and flashes as far as the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man.
Wherever there is a carcass, there the vultures will gather.

units.label: The coming of the Son of Man
units.note: on the clouds with power and glory

verses.text: Immediately after the tribulation of those days: ‘The sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light; the stars will fall from the sky, and the powers of the heavens will be shaken.’
At that time the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven, and all the tribes of the earth will mourn. They will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory.
And He will send out His angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather His elect from the four winds, from one end of the heavens to the other.

units.label: The lesson of the fig tree
units.note: this generation and My abiding word

verses.text: Now learn this lesson from the fig tree: As soon as its branches become tender and sprout leaves, you know that summer is near.
So also, when you see all these things, you will know that He is near, right at the door.
Truly I tell you, this generation will not pass away until all these things have happened.
Heaven and earth will pass away, but My words will never pass away.

units.label: Keep watch
units.note: no one knows the day or hour

verses.text: No one knows about that day or hour, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.
As it was in the days of Noah, so will it be at the coming of the Son of Man.
For in the days before the flood, people were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, up to the day Noah entered the ark.
And they were oblivious until the flood came and swept them all away. So will it be at the coming of the Son of Man.
Two men will be in the field: one will be taken and the other left.
Two women will be grinding at the mill: one will be taken and the other left.
Therefore {{gregoreo|keep watch}}, because you do not know the day on which your Lord will come.
But understand this: If the homeowner had known in which watch of the night the thief was coming, he would have kept watch and would not have let his house be broken into.
For this reason, you also must be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour you do not expect.

units.label: The faithful and the wicked servant

verses.text: Who then is the faithful and wise servant, whom the master has put in charge of his household, to give the others their food at the proper time?
Blessed is that servant whose master finds him doing so when he returns.
Truly I tell you, he will put him in charge of all his possessions.
But suppose that servant is wicked and says in his heart, ‘My master will be away a long time.’
And he begins to beat his fellow servants and to eat and drink with drunkards.
The master of that servant will come on a day he does not expect and at an hour he does not anticipate.
Then he will cut him to pieces and assign him a place with the hypocrites, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.

parousia.gloss: Presence, arrival, coming. Used of the royal visit of a king, it becomes the New Testament word for Christ's return in glory. The disciples ask for the sign of His parousia, and Jesus answers that it will be sudden, visible, and unmistakable, like lightning across the sky.

thlipsis.gloss: Pressure, affliction, tribulation. Jesus foretells a great tribulation, a distress connected with the fall of Jerusalem and, many hold, with the sufferings of the whole age before His return; yet for the sake of the elect those days are cut short.

gregoreo.gloss: To stay awake, be alert, keep watch. Because the day is unknown, the command is not to calculate but to watch, living ready and faithful, like a household expecting its master at any hour.

basic.context: Leaving the temple, Jesus foretells its total destruction. On the Mount of Olives the disciples ask when this will happen and what will signal His coming and the end of the age. Jesus warns them not to be deceived by false messiahs, wars, and disasters, which are only the beginning. He speaks of persecution, worldwide gospel witness, a great tribulation centered on Jerusalem, and finally His own glorious, visible return on the clouds. Because no one knows the day or hour, He calls His people to watch and to serve faithfully, like a servant found at his work when the master returns.
basic.hermeneutics: The disciples ask a double question, about the temple's fall and about the end, and much of the difficulty is that Jesus answers both, sometimes together. Some sayings clearly point to AD 70, others to His final return. The consistent thrust is pastoral: do not be deceived, do not set dates, persevere, and keep watch. Read the timing debates humbly, holding the certainties firmly.
basic.originalLanguages: The disciples ask for the sign of His parousia, His royal coming. Jesus foretells a great thlipsis, tribulation, and then, because the hour is hidden, commands His people to gregoreo, to stay awake and keep watch.
basic.typology: The abomination of desolation draws on Daniel; the darkened sun and shaken heavens draw on the prophets' day of the Lord. The Son of Man coming on the clouds fulfills Daniel 7. The days of Noah become a pattern of judgment falling on the unready.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Son of Man of Daniel 7 who will come on the clouds with power and great glory to gather His elect. He is the truthful prophet whose words will never pass away, and the returning Master before whom every servant will give account.
basic.god: God rules over history's upheavals, which must happen but do not signal the end by themselves. He sets the day and hour by His own authority, cuts short the days for the elect's sake, and will surely gather His people and judge the world.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man on the clouds. Daniel 9 and 11 give the abomination of desolation. Isaiah 13 and Joel 2 give the darkened heavens. 1 Thessalonians 4 and 5 echo the trumpet, the gathering, and the thief in the night.
basic.oneStory: As the passion nears, Jesus lifts His disciples' eyes past the cross and the fall of Jerusalem to the horizon of history: His own return in glory to gather His people and judge the world. Between now and then lies a long age of witness, tribulation, and deception, through which His people are to persevere and keep watch. The story of the kingdom, hidden now, will end in the visible, glorious coming of the Son of Man.

context.literary: This is Matthew's fifth and final discourse, delivered privately on the Mount of Olives facing the temple, and it flows directly from the lament and the your house is left desolate of chapter 23. Chapter 24 addresses the signs and the coming; chapter 25 adds three parables on readiness and judgment. The discourse closes Jesus' teaching before the passion narrative begins in chapter 26.
context.historical: Herod's temple was a wonder of white stone and gold; its predicted destruction was staggering, and it was fulfilled when Rome razed Jerusalem in AD 70. The abomination of desolation recalls Antiochus Epiphanes desecrating the temple in 167 BC and points to a further desecration. Fleeing to the mountains, rooftop escape, and winter or Sabbath flight all reflect concrete first-century realities of the Judean war.
context.canonical: The discourse is woven from the prophets: Daniel 7:13-14 (the Son of Man on the clouds), Daniel 9:27 and 11:31 (the abomination), Isaiah 13:10 and 34:4 and Joel 2:10 (the darkened heavens), and Zechariah 12 (the tribes mourning). It shapes later New Testament expectation in 1 Thessalonians 4-5, 2 Thessalonians 2, and Revelation, the gospel to all nations, the trumpet, and the thief in the night.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer the disciples' question about the temple and the end by warning against deception and date-setting, foretelling tribulation and the certain, glorious return of the Son of Man, and calling His people to persevere, watch, and serve faithfully.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The predictions describe what will unfold, warning against false alarms and premature panic. The prescriptions are clear and repeated: do not be deceived, do not fall away, persevere to the end, keep watch, and be the faithful servant found at his work.

debates.question: Do the disciples' two questions, about the temple and about the end, have one answer or two?

views.label: Largely one intertwined answer
views.case: The disciples assumed the temple's fall and the end of the age coincided, and Jesus answers the whole, so much of the discourse describes AD 70 as a foreshadowing that flows into the final coming, with this generation seeing the temple destroyed.

views.label: Two distinguishable horizons
views.case: Jesus deliberately separates them: some sayings (flee Judea, this generation) fit the AD 70 crisis, while others (the visible coming on the clouds, no one knows the day) point beyond it to His final return, so the discourse moves from the near event to the far one.

debates.bearing: Either way the near judgment on Jerusalem and the far, glorious return are both real; the fall of the temple is a genuine fulfillment and a pattern that assures the certainty of the final coming.

debates.question: What does this generation will not pass away mean in verse 34?

views.label: The generation that saw the fall of Jerusalem
views.case: Taken naturally, this generation is Jesus' contemporaries, who indeed witnessed the temple's destruction within forty years, so all these things refers primarily to the events climaxing in AD 70.

views.label: The generation that sees the final signs, or the Jewish people
views.case: Others read this generation as the future generation that witnesses the end-time signs immediately preceding the coming, or as the enduring Jewish people preserved until the end, so the saying guarantees fulfillment without dating the return.

debates.bearing: Both guard Jesus' reliability and His refusal to let anyone set the day; whether centered on AD 70 or the final signs, His words stand, and heaven and earth will pass away before they fail.

study.originalLanguages: The disciples ask for the sign of His parousia, the royal arrival of the Son of Man, and of the end of the age. Jesus refuses to feed date-setting; instead He warns of deception and foretells a great thlipsis, a tribulation bound up with Jerusalem's fall and the sufferings of the age, cut short for the elect's sake. His coming, He says, will be no secret rumor in the wilderness but sudden and visible as lightning, the Son of Man on the clouds with power and great glory. And because the day and hour are known only to the Father, the repeated command is gregoreo, keep watch, stay awake, and be found faithful, for the Master will come at an hour no one expects.

typology.type: The Son of Man on the clouds
typology.fulfillment: Daniel 7:13-14, the one given everlasting dominion, fulfilled in Christ's return in glory to gather His elect.
Daniel's desecration, echoed in the profaning of the temple and its destruction, and a pattern of end-time rebellion, 2 Thessalonians 2:3-4.
Sudden judgment on an oblivious world, a pattern for the coming of the Son of Man, Genesis 7, 2 Peter 3:3-7.

study.christ: Jesus is the Son of Man of Daniel's vision who will come on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory, sending His angels with a trumpet to gather His elect from the ends of the earth. He is the truthful prophet whose predictions unfold in history, whose words will outlast heaven and earth, and who, in His humanity, submits even His knowledge of the day to the Father. He is the returning Master before whom every servant gives account, and the Judge who separates the faithful from the wicked. The cross and resurrection stand between His first coming and this glorious return, and they secure the certainty of it.
study.god: God is sovereign over the whole sweep of history, over wars, disasters, and the rise and fall of nations, none of which escape His hand or force His timetable. He alone knows the day and hour, keeping it in His own authority, and He cuts short the days of tribulation for the sake of His chosen. He is the God of the covenant who preserves and gathers His elect through every deception and trial, and He will bring history to its appointed end in the visible coming of His Son, when the nations mourn and the redeemed are gathered home.

crossReferences.note: The Son of Man coming with the clouds, given dominion, glory, and a kingdom, fulfilled in Christ's return.
The abomination that causes desolation, referenced by Jesus for the coming desecration.
The Lord's coming with a trumpet call and the gathering of His people, echoing verse 31.
Scoffers, the delay, and the sudden day of the Lord like a thief, developing the watchfulness theme.

study.oneStory: On the eve of His passion, Jesus lifts His disciples' gaze beyond the cross and the fall of Jerusalem to the end of the story: His own return in glory as the Son of Man to gather His people and judge the world. Between now and then stretches a long age of gospel witness, tribulation, and deception, through which His people persevere and keep watch. The kingdom, hidden and contested now, will be consummated when He comes on the clouds. Until that unknown day, the call is not to calculate but to be found faithful at our post.

quiz.q: How does Jesus tell His disciples to respond to wars, disasters, and false messiahs?

options.text: Not to be deceived or alarmed, since these are only the beginning of birth pains, not the end.
options.note: Verses 4-8 warn against deception and panic; such things do not yet mean the end.

options.text: To recognize these as the certain sign that the end has arrived.
options.note: Jesus says the opposite, that the end is still to come; these are only beginnings.

options.text: To follow those who claim to be the Christ.
options.note: He warns explicitly not to believe such claims.

options.text: To stop all gospel witness until He returns.
options.note: He says the gospel must be preached to all nations before the end.

quiz.q: What will the coming of the Son of Man be like?

options.text: Sudden, visible, and glorious, like lightning across the sky, seen by all.
options.note: Verses 27-30 describe an unmistakable, public coming on the clouds with power and glory.

options.text: A powerful event that His followers will witness.
options.note: True, but it understates that all the tribes of the earth will see it; it is public, not private.

options.text: A secret appearance in the wilderness or inner rooms.
options.note: Jesus warns against exactly such secret-location claims as false.

options.text: An event no one will ever be able to perceive.
options.note: It will be visible to all, like lightning from east to west.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say about the timing of His return?

options.text: No one knows the day or hour except the Father, so His people must keep watch.
options.note: Verses 36 and 42 place the day in the Father's knowledge and command watchfulness.

options.text: It can be calculated from the signs He describes.
options.note: The signs prepare His people, but He insists the day and hour are unknown, ruling out calculation.

options.text: It will come only after a long, predictable delay.
options.note: He warns against the servant who presumes a long delay; the hour is unexpected.

options.text: It will happen at a time everyone will expect.
options.note: He says it will come at an hour you do not expect.

quiz.q: What distinguishes the faithful servant from the wicked one?

options.text: The faithful servant is found doing his master's work; the wicked one presumes delay and mistreats others.
options.note: Verses 45-51 contrast faithful readiness with presumptuous negligence.

options.text: The faithful servant knows exactly when the master will return.
options.note: Neither knows the time; the difference is faithfulness, not secret knowledge.

options.text: The wicked servant simply forgot the master was coming.
options.note: He actively presumed a long delay and abused others; it was not mere forgetfulness.

options.text: Both servants are treated the same in the end.
options.note: One is rewarded, the other assigned a place with the hypocrites.

quiz.q: What does Jesus promise about His own words in verse 35?

options.text: Heaven and earth will pass away, but His words will never pass away.
options.note: Verse 35 affirms the permanence and reliability of Jesus' words.

options.text: His words will endure as long as the present world lasts.
options.note: He claims more; His words outlast heaven and earth themselves, which this limits.

options.text: His predictions apply only to the generation He addressed.
options.note: His words abide forever; even the this generation saying serves that certainty.

options.text: His words may fail if the events are delayed.
options.note: He guarantees the opposite, that His words will never fail.

questions: Jesus repeatedly warns, do not be deceived. What voices or claims are most likely to pull you off course, and how are you guarding against them?
The love of many will grow cold, yet the one who perseveres will be saved. Where do you feel your love cooling, and what would perseverance look like this season?
No one knows the day or hour. How does refusing to speculate about dates actually free you to live faithfully today?
The faithful servant is simply found doing his work when the master returns. If Christ returned this week, what would He find you doing?
The return of Christ will be visible and glorious. How does the certainty of that day steady you amid the upheavals and fears of the present one?

translationNotes.note: The abomination of desolation alludes to Daniel 9:27, 11:31, and 12:11; let the reader understand signals a deliberate pointer to Daniel and to the desecration and destruction of the temple.
The darkened sun and moon and falling stars are prophetic day-of-the-LORD imagery from Isaiah 13:10, Isaiah 34:4, and Joel 2:10; presented here as prose for a clean, dash-free reading.
Some manuscripts omit nor the Son; where included, it expresses that in His incarnate life Jesus voluntarily did not know the day, which the Father keeps in His own authority.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 24:1-51. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, poem, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
